<a name="Conduit"></a>
## type Conduit

Conduit\[T\] pairs a channel of T with its cleanup so callers don't have to hold both separately. The close function is type\-specific and supplied at construction time.

```go
type Conduit[T any] struct {
    // contains filtered or unexported fields
}
```

<a name="FileWatcher"></a>
### func FileWatcher

```go
func FileWatcher(paths ...string) *Conduit[notify.EventInfo]
```

FileWatcher returns a Conduit\[notify.EventInfo\] that watches each of the given absolute paths \(non\-recursive\) and forwards create, write, remove, and rename events. Close\(\) stops all watches.

<a name="FileWatcherRecursive"></a>
### func FileWatcherRecursive

```go
func FileWatcherRecursive(paths ...string) *Conduit[notify.EventInfo]
```

FileWatcherRecursive is like FileWatcher but watches each path recursively, including all subdirectories.

<a name="Map"></a>
### func Map

```go
func Map[T, U any](w *Conduit[T], fn func(T) U) *Conduit[U]
```

Map returns a new Conduit that transforms each event with fn. The goroutine exits when the input channel closes. Close\(\) propagates to the upstream Conduit.

<a name="MapWithState"></a>
### func MapWithState

```go
func MapWithState[T, S, U any](w *Conduit[T], initial S, fn func(S, T) (S, U)) *Conduit[U]
```

MapWithState returns a Conduit\[U\] that transforms each event using fn, which carries a state value S across calls. fn receives the current state and the incoming event and returns the next state and the output event to forward.

<a name="Null"></a>
### func Null

```go
func Null[T any]() *Conduit[T]
```

Null returns a Conduit that never emits. Its channel is nil, so a select case on Events\(\) blocks forever — equivalent to the conduit being absent. Close is a no\-op.

<a name="OneOf"></a>
### func OneOf

```go
func OneOf[T any](conduits ...*Conduit[T]) *Conduit[T]
```

OneOf returns a Conduit\[T\] that forwards events from any of the given conduits. The output channel receives whichever event arrives first. All upstream conduits are closed when the returned Conduit is closed. The goroutine exits when all input channels close.

<a name="Ticker"></a>
### func Ticker

```go
func Ticker(d time.Duration) *Conduit[time.Time]
```

Ticker returns a Conduit\[time.Time\] that emits the current time at every interval d. Close\(\) stops the underlying ticker and exits the goroutine.

<a name="Conduit[T].Close"></a>
### func \(\*Conduit\[T\]\) Close

```go
func (w *Conduit[T]) Close()
```

Close runs the cleanup function supplied at construction.

<a name="Conduit[T].Events"></a>
### func \(\*Conduit\[T\]\) Events

```go
func (w *Conduit[T]) Events() <-chan T
```

Events returns the receive\-only channel of events.

<a name="Conduit[T].Filter"></a>
### func \(\*Conduit\[T\]\) Filter

```go
func (w *Conduit[T]) Filter(keep func(T) bool) *Conduit[T]
```

Filter returns a new Conduit that passes through only events for which keep returns true. The goroutine exits when the input channel closes. Close\(\) propagates to the upstream Conduit.

<a name="Conduit[T].Throttle"></a>
### func \(\*Conduit\[T\]\) Throttle

```go
func (w *Conduit[T]) Throttle(d time.Duration) *Conduit[struct{}]
```

Throttle returns a Conduit\[struct\{\}\] that drains events for d after the first event arrives, then emits one signal \(trailing\-edge, fixed window\). This absorbs the burst of filesystem events that typically follows a single user action before notifying the caller. The goroutine exits when the input channel closes. Close\(\) propagates to the upstream Conduit.
